addedBlocks:
## claude.ai Honeycomb
Honeycomb MCP Server - Observability Data Query Interface

<role>
You are an observability assistant for Honeycomb users. You investigate production behavior — performance, errors, deployment impact, alert state — by querying real telemetry through these tools. The workspace's actual state is only knowable through tool calls; do not reason from priors about what services, datasets, or alerts exist.

When the user names a dataset, span, or service, treat it as a starting point for discovery, not a license to skip it. The right span name within a dataset is rarely obvious from the dataset name alone, and aggregating the wrong attribute or the wrong span is the most common cause of misleading answers.
</role>

<workflow>
For most observability questions — including "how many", "what's slow", "what's erroring" — start with span discovery (list_spans, get_span_details). These tools return counts and attribute distributions directly; they often answer the question without a follow-up query. Reach for run_query only when you need a calculation, distribution, or comparison those tools can't express. Span tools work across all trace-aware datasets in scope without needing to know which dataset to target.

1. ORIENT
   - Call 'get_workspace_context' to learn the team, environments, and dataset counts. Use the returned environment slug in subsequent calls.

2. DISCOVER (pick the path that matches the question)
   - Span discovery — for "what's happening / what's slow / how many X / what does service X do":
     - 'list_spans' ranks span names by count with root_count to distinguish entry points from internal helpers. Counts here are often the answer to "how many X happened".
     - 'get_span_details' on a chosen span_name returns its populated attributes, value cardinality, and top observed values — including which spans actually populate a count-bearing attribute.
     Answer directly from these when you can — composing an equivalent run_query by hand is slower and more error-prone.
   - Alert / SLO state — for "what's bro… [truncated]

## claude.ai Linear
When passing string values to tools, send the content directly without escape sequences. For example, use real newlines in markdown content rather than literal backslash-n (\n) characters.

attachment.content:
- changelog: Generate or update the CHANGELOG.md for a new release version. Use when the user says "generate changelog", "update changelog", "write release notes", or asks to prepare a changelog for a version like "changelog for 0.5.3".
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- cloudflare:build-agent: Build an AI agent on Cloudflare using the Agents SDK
- cloudflare:build-mcp: Build a remote MCP server on Cloudflare using McpAgent
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- cloudflare:agents-sdk: Build AI agents on Cloudflare Workers using the Agents SDK. Load when creating stateful agents, durable workflows, real-time WebSocket apps, scheduled tasks, MCP servers, chat applications, voice agents, or browser automation. Covers Agent class, state management, callable RPC, Workflows, durable execution, queues, retries, observability, and React hooks. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare:cloudflare: Comprehensive Cloudflare platform skill covering Workers, Pages, storage (KV, D1, R2), AI (Workers AI, Vectorize, Agents SDK), feature flags (Flagship), networking (Tunnel, Spectrum), security (WAF, DDoS), and infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, Pulumi). Use for any Cloudflare development task. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare:cloudflare-email-service: Send and receive transactional emails with Cloudflare Email Service (Email Sending + Email Routing). Use when building email sending (Workers binding or REST API), email routing, Agents SDK email handling, or integrating email into any app — Workers, Node.js, Python, Go, etc. Also use for email deliverability, SPF/DKIM/DMARC, wrangler email setup, MCP email tools, or when a coding agent needs to send emails. Even for simple requests like "add email to my Worker" — this skill has critical config details.
- cloudflare:durable-objects: Create and review Cloudflare Durable Objects. Use when building stateful coordination (chat rooms, multiplayer games, booking systems), implementing RPC methods, SQLite storage, alarms, WebSockets, or reviewing DO code for best practices. Covers Workers integration, wrangler config, and testing with Vitest. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare:sandbox-sdk: Build sandboxed applications for secure code execution. Load when building AI code execution, code interpreters, CI/CD systems, interactive dev environments, or executing untrusted code. Covers Sandbox SDK lifecycle, commands, files, code interpreter, and preview URLs. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare:web-perf: Analyzes web performance using Chrome DevTools MCP. Measures Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) and supplementary metrics (FCP, TBT, Speed Index), identifies render-blocking resources, network dependency chains, layout shifts, caching issues, and accessibility gaps. Use when asked to audit, profile, debug, or optimize page load performance, Lighthouse scores, or site speed. Biases towards retrieval from current documentation over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare:workers-best-practices: Reviews and authors Cloudflare Workers code against production best practices. Load when writing new Workers, reviewing Worker code, configuring wrangler.jsonc, or checking for common Workers anti-patterns (streaming, floating promises, global state, secrets, bindings, observability). Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare:wrangler: Cloudflare Workers CLI for deploying, developing, and managing Workers, KV, R2, D1, Vectorize, Hyperdrive, Workers AI, Containers, Queues, Workflows, Pipelines, and Secrets Store. Load before running wrangler commands to ensure correct syntax and best practices. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
=== PR #66 body + meta ===
{
    "additions": 4003,
    "body": "## Summary

New `git-sync convert-sha256` subcommand. One-off conversion: fetches a pack over smart HTTP from a SHA1 source, walks every reachable object, and writes a fresh SHA256 bare repository where every tree/commit/tag reference is re-encoded under SHA256. All branches and tags are always converted (partial scope risks stranding cross-branch references in messages). Behavior, sharp edges, and operational characteristics documented in `docs/convert-sha256.md`.

## Design: two-pass topological DFS

The translation needs an authoritative "what's in scope" view *before* encoding starts, so abbreviated SHA1 prefixes in commit messages get the same uniqueness verdict regardless of how far translation has progressed, and so cross-branch message references (cherry-picks, reverts, etc.) can be added as dependency edges in the DFS:

1. **Discovery**: a non-encoding DFS from each desired ref tip walks every reachable object via tree entries, commit `tree`/`parent` links, and tag targets. Produces a `reachable map[SHA1]ObjectType` covering the full in-scope set. Submodule gitlinks pointing outside the repo error *here*, before the target bare repo is materialized — failing fast keeps half-converted state off disk.
2. **Translation**: memoized recursive DFS in topological order. Tree, parent, tag-target, *and message-reference* edges are all part of the DFS, so the SHA1 → SHA256 mapping contains every dependency by the time any object's bytes are encoded. Single forward sweep — no cascade re-encoding, no per-commit dirty bookkeeping.

Cycles in the combined dependency graph are cryptographically infeasible (forming a cycle via message references would require knowing each hash before computing it), so the topological order is always valid. A defensive `inProgress` guard turns unexpected graph shapes into a clear error rather than a stack overflow.

Loose objects are written by hand — `go-git@v6.0.0-alpha.3`'s `objfile.Writer` hardcodes SHA1 in its hasher, so the standard `SetEncodedObject` path would place every translated object at a SHA1-derived filename even on a SHA256 store. A unit test recomputes `sha256` of every loose object's decompressed content and compares against the filename to prevent regression.

## Signing (`--sign`)

After conversion, `--sign` shells out to `git tag -s converted/<branch> <tip>` for every converted branch. Each resulting signed annotated tag transitively attests the entire reachable history of that branch via the parent chain: tampering with any historical commit changes ancestor hashes, which changes the tip's hash, which changes the tag's `object` line, which invalidates the signature.

The signature is by the *converter*, not by the original authors — the originals' signatures are necessarily lost (they signed over pre-rewrite bytes). For internal mirrors and single-identity repos that's a strict improvement over unsigned-everywhere; for broad public repos it's weaker than the pre-conversion chain. SSH signing (`gpg.format = ssh`) and OpenPGP both work — we delegate to `git`, so whatever the user's git config picks is honored. `--sign-key <id>` overrides the default key.

## Example output

`--sign --check` against `entireio/cli` (283 refs, 6,950 commits, 55,289 objects):

```
$ OUT=$(mktemp -d) && ./git-sync convert-sha256 --sign --check https://github.com/entireio/cli.git "$OUT"
fetching 283 ref(s) from https://github.com/entireio/cli.git ...
discovering reachable objects ...
translating objects to sha256 ...
signing refs/tags/converted/1072-intermittent-stop-hook-timeout-... ...
signing refs/tags/converted/20260116-git-hook-absolute-path ...
signing refs/tags/converted/20260119-do-not-pin-go ...
... (one line per branch tip)
verifying output ...
  ✓ config: extensions.objectformat = sha256
  ✓ HEAD: 11a78c5d7ddc121d551d9bbf5e8fe7de2b329da77334cde2f2131ec44b1ef83d
  ✓ refs: 283 / 283 resolve to objects
  ✓ git fsck --full: clean
sha256 bare repo: /var/folders/.../tmp.S5PP3dNay8
source: https://github.com/entireio/cli.git (v2)
converted: 22989 blobs, 25350 trees, 6950 commits, 0 tags
refs written: 283
warning: stripped 4042 GPG signature(s); they no longer match the rewritten object content
rewrote 48 SHA1 hash reference(s) in commit/tag messages
origin notes ref: refs/notes/sha1-origin (use `git notes --ref=sha1-origin show <sha256>` to recover old SHA1)
signed 213 branch attestation tag(s): refs/tags/converted/1072-..., refs/tags/converted/20260116-..., refs/tags/converted/20260119-..., refs/tags/converted/20260128-..., refs/tags/converted/20260206-..., ... (208 more; full list in --json)
```

Inspecting one of the signed tags:

```
$ git -C "$OUT" cat-file -p refs/tags/converted/main
object 11a78c5d7ddc121d551d9bbf5e8fe7de2b329da77334cde2f2131ec44b1ef83d
type commit
tag converted/main
tagger Andrea Nodari <and.nodari@gmail.com> 1779698132 +0200

SHA1 → SHA256 conversion attestation for refs/heads/main.

Source: https://github.com/entireio/cli.git
Produced by git-sync convert-sha256.
-----BEGIN SSH SIGNATURE-----
REDACTED
PowQqx6UX8bp0evB4AAAADZ2l0AAAAAAAAAAZzaGE1MTIAAABTAAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5
AAAAQLn2hdEdYiTF5MFug8L+mVD6fU/REDACTED
uk/lW8VIW/iErg9K46vA8=
-----END SSH SIGNATURE-----
```

`git verify-tag refs/tags/converted/main` (against the user's allowed-signers file) then verifies the entire history.

## Other flags

- `--all-refs` / `--exclude-ref-prefix` — extend scope to notes/pulls/etc., or subtract
- `--write-mapping <path>` — emit SHA1 → SHA256 TSV for rewriting external systems (PR descriptions, deploy manifests, etc.)
- `--no-rewrite-messages` — skip inline rewriting of hash references in commit/tag messages
- `--no-origin-notes` — skip `refs/notes/sha1-origin` (per-commit reverse lookup of original SHA1)
- `--check` — post-conversion sanity steps (config, HEAD resolves, refs resolve, `git fsck --full`)
- `--progress` — live per-phase object counts (TTY only)
- `--sign-key <id>` — override `user.signingkey` for `--sign`

Full reference in [`docs/convert-sha256.md`](docs/convert-sha256.md).

## Test plan

- [x] `go test ./...` passes
- [x] `go test -race ./cmd/git-sync/internal/sha256convert/` clean (atomic counters used so the `--progress` ticker doesn't race against translation)
- [x] Unit tests cover the translator end-to-end: blobs/trees/commits/tags including signed commit + signed annotated tag, idempotency, on-disk `sha256(content)` invariant for every loose object, message rewriting (ancestor + cross-branch + ambiguous + skip), origin notes, mapping file, submodule fail-fast in discovery
- [x] Integration test against local `git-http-backend` (gated on `GITSYNC_E2E_SHA256_HTTP_BACKEND=1`): exercises pack fetch + discovery + translation + rewriting + notes + mapping + `--check` end-to-end, asserts `git fsck` clean
- [x] Signing integration test: spins up an ephemeral ed25519 SSH key via `ssh-keygen`, points `GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL` at a generated gitconfig with SSH signing, runs `convert-sha256 --sign`, asserts the resulting `refs/tags/converted/main` is a signed annotated tag targeting the converted branch tip

=== PR #66 body + meta ===
{
    "additions": 4003,
    "body": "## Summary

New `git-sync convert-sha256` subcommand. One-off conversion: fetches a pack over smart HTTP from a SHA1 source, walks every reachable object, and writes a fresh SHA256 bare repository where every tree/commit/tag reference is re-encoded under SHA256. All branches and tags are always converted (partial scope risks stranding cross-branch references in messages). Behavior, sharp edges, and operational characteristics documented in `docs/convert-sha256.md`.

## Design: two-pass topological DFS

The translation needs an authoritative "what's in scope" view *before* encoding starts, so abbreviated SHA1 prefixes in commit messages get the same uniqueness verdict regardless of how far translation has progressed, and so cross-branch message references (cherry-picks, reverts, etc.) can be added as dependency edges in the DFS:

1. **Discovery**: a non-encoding DFS from each desired ref tip walks every reachable object via tree entries, commit `tree`/`parent` links, and tag targets. Produces a `reachable map[SHA1]ObjectType` covering the full in-scope set. Submodule gitlinks pointing outside the repo error *here*, before the target bare repo is materialized — failing fast keeps half-converted state off disk.
2. **Translation**: memoized recursive DFS in topological order. Tree, parent, tag-target, *and message-reference* edges are all part of the DFS, so the SHA1 → SHA256 mapping contains every dependency by the time any object's bytes are encoded. Single forward sweep — no cascade re-encoding, no per-commit dirty bookkeeping.

Cycles in the combined dependency graph are cryptographically infeasible (forming a cycle via message references would require knowing each hash before computing it), so the topological order is always valid. A defensive `inProgress` guard turns unexpected graph shapes into a clear error rather than a stack overflow.

Loose objects are written by hand — `go-git@v6.0.0-alpha.3`'s `objfile.Writer` hardcodes SHA1 in its hasher, so the standard `SetEncodedObject` path would place every translated object at a SHA1-derived filename even on a SHA256 store. A unit test recomputes `sha256` of every loose object's decompressed content and compares against the filename to prevent regression.

## Signing (`--sign`)

After conversion, `--sign` shells out to `git tag -s converted/<branch> <tip>` for every converted branch. Each resulting signed annotated tag transitively attests the entire reachable history of that branch via the parent chain: tampering with any historical commit changes ancestor hashes, which changes the tip's hash, which changes the tag's `object` line, which invalidates the signature.

The signature is by the *converter*, not by the original authors — the originals' signatures are necessarily lost (they signed over pre-rewrite bytes). For internal mirrors and single-identity repos that's a strict improvement over unsigned-everywhere; for broad public repos it's weaker than the pre-conversion chain. SSH signing (`gpg.format = ssh`) and OpenPGP both work — we delegate to `git`, so whatever the user's git config picks is honored. `--sign-key <id>` overrides the default key.

## Example output

`--sign --check` against `entireio/cli` (283 refs, 6,950 commits, 55,289 objects):

```
$ OUT=$(mktemp -d) && ./git-sync convert-sha256 --sign --check https://github.com/entireio/cli.git "$OUT"
fetching 283 ref(s) from https://github.com/entireio/cli.git ...
discovering reachable objects ...
translating objects to sha256 ...
signing refs/tags/converted/1072-intermittent-stop-hook-timeout-... ...
signing refs/tags/converted/20260116-git-hook-absolute-path ...
signing refs/tags/converted/20260119-do-not-pin-go ...
... (one line per branch tip)
verifying output ...
  ✓ config: extensions.objectformat = sha256
  ✓ HEAD: 11a78c5d7ddc121d551d9bbf5e8fe7de2b329da77334cde2f2131ec44b1ef83d
  ✓ refs: 283 / 283 resolve to objects
  ✓ git fsck --full: clean
sha256 bare repo: /var/folders/.../tmp.S5PP3dNay8
source: https://github.com/entireio/cli.git (v2)
converted: 22989 blobs, 25350 trees, 6950 commits, 0 tags
refs written: 283
warning: stripped 4042 GPG signature(s); they no longer match the rewritten object content
rewrote 48 SHA1 hash reference(s) in commit/tag messages
origin notes ref: refs/notes/sha1-origin (use `git notes --ref=sha1-origin show <sha256>` to recover old SHA1)
signed 213 branch attestation tag(s): refs/tags/converted/1072-..., refs/tags/converted/20260116-..., refs/tags/converted/20260119-..., refs/tags/converted/20260128-..., refs/tags/converted/20260206-..., ... (208 more; full list in --json)
```

Inspecting one of the signed tags:

```
$ git -C "$OUT" cat-file -p refs/tags/converted/main
object 11a78c5d7ddc121d551d9bbf5e8fe7de2b329da77334cde2f2131ec44b1ef83d
type commit
tag converted/main
tagger Andrea Nodari <and.nodari@gmail.com> 1779698132 +0200

SHA1 → SHA256 conversion attestation for refs/heads/main.

Source: https://github.com/entireio/cli.git
Produced by git-sync convert-sha256.
-----BEGIN SSH SIGNATURE-----
REDACTED
PowQqx6UX8bp0evB4AAAADZ2l0AAAAAAAAAAZzaGE1MTIAAABTAAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5
AAAAQLn2hdEdYiTF5MFug8L+mVD6fU/REDACTED
uk/lW8VIW/iErg9K46vA8=
-----END SSH SIGNATURE-----
```

`git verify-tag refs/tags/converted/main` (against the user's allowed-signers file) then verifies the entire history.

## Other flags

- `--all-refs` / `--exclude-ref-prefix` — extend scope to notes/pulls/etc., or subtract
- `--write-mapping <path>` — emit SHA1 → SHA256 TSV for rewriting external systems (PR descriptions, deploy manifests, etc.)
- `--no-rewrite-messages` — skip inline rewriting of hash references in commit/tag messages
- `--no-origin-notes` — skip `refs/notes/sha1-origin` (per-commit reverse lookup of original SHA1)
- `--check` — post-conversion sanity steps (config, HEAD resolves, refs resolve, `git fsck --full`)
- `--progress` — live per-phase object counts (TTY only)
- `--sign-key <id>` — override `user.signingkey` for `--sign`

Full reference in [`docs/convert-sha256.md`](docs/convert-sha256.md).

## Test plan

- [x] `go test ./...` passes
- [x] `go test -race ./cmd/git-sync/internal/sha256convert/` clean (atomic counters used so the `--progress` ticker doesn't race against translation)
- [x] Unit tests cover the translator end-to-end: blobs/trees/commits/tags including signed commit + signed annotated tag, idempotency, on-disk `sha256(content)` invariant for every loose object, message rewriting (ancestor + cross-branch + ambiguous + skip), origin notes, mapping file, submodule fail-fast in discovery
- [x] Integration test against local `git-http-backend` (gated on `GITSYNC_E2E_SHA256_HTTP_BACKEND=1`): exercises pack fetch + discovery + translation + rewriting + notes + mapping + `--check` end-to-end, asserts `git fsck` clean
- [x] Signing integration test: spins up an ephemeral ed25519 SSH key via `ssh-keygen`, points `GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL` at a generated gitconfig with SSH signing, runs `convert-sha256 --sign`, asserts the resulting `refs/tags/converted/main` is a signed annotated tag targeting the converted branch tip
- [x] Smoke-tested manually against `spf13/cobra` (37 refs, ~4,500 objects) and `entireio/cli` (283 refs, ~55,000 objects); `git fsck --full` clean; `git verify-tag refs/tags/converted/main` passes against an SSH allowed-signers file

content.content:
1	package main
2	
3	import (
4		"errors"
5		"fmt"
6	
7		gitsync "entire.io/entire/git-sync"
8		"entire.io/entire/git-sync/cmd/git-sync/internal/sha256convert"
9		"github.com/spf13/cobra"
10	)
11	
12	func newConvertSHA256Cmd() *cobra.Command {
13		var (
14			req         = sha256convert.Request{}
15			jsonOutput  bool
16			protocolVal = newProtocolFlag()
17		)
18	
19		cmd := &cobra.Command{
20			Use:   "convert-sha256 [flags] <source-url> <target-dir>",
21			Short: "One-off SHA1 → SHA256 conversion of a remote repo into a local bare repo",
22			Long: `convert-sha256 fetches a pack from a SHA1 HTTP source and writes a new
23	SHA256 bare repository on disk at <target-dir>. Every reachable object is
24	re-hashed under SHA256 and tree/commit/tag references are rewritten.
25	
26	All branches and tags on the source are always converted — partial scope
27	risks stranding cross-branch references in commit messages. Pass
28	--all-refs to also include refs/notes/* and other custom namespaces;
29	pass --exclude-ref-prefix to subtract specific namespaces from --all-refs.
30	Exclude prefixes that would drop any branch or tag (e.g. refs/heads/feature/,
31	refs/tags/, refs/) are rejected at run time to preserve the always-convert
32	invariant.
33	
34	Server-internal pull/merge-request refs (refs/pull/*, refs/pull-requests/*,
35	refs/merge-requests/*) are NOT converted even under --all-refs: they hold
36	unmerged code foreign to the repository, and mirroring the result onward
37	would republish it. Pass --include-pull-refs to convert them anyway.
38	
39	The conversion is destructive in two ways the caller should be aware of:
40	GPG signatures on commits and tags are dropped (they sign over the
41	original SHA1 content and would be invalid post-rewrite), and any
42	submodule gitlink fails the run — its .gitmodules upstream still
43	advertises SHA1 hashes, so a rewritten SHA256 gitlink would point at a
44	hash the upstream cannot resolve and break ` + "`git submodule update`" + ` in
45	every clone. Exclude refs that reference submodules, or convert the
46	submodule repository first and re-point .gitmodules.`,
47			Args:          cobra.MaximumNArgs(2),
48			SilenceErrors: true,
49			SilenceUsage:  true,
50			RunE: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
51				req.ProtocolMode = gitsync.ProtocolMode(protocolVal)
52				if err := resolveConvertSHA256Args(&req, args); err != nil {
53					return err
54				}
55	
56				result, err := sha256convert.Run(cmd.Context(), req)
57				// Print whatever state Run produced even on error: signed
58				// tags landed before signBranchTips failed, --check
59				// findings, and the --keep-source-objects temp dir are
60				// all things the user needs to see to clean up or debug.
61				// Run zero-values fields it never touched, so this is
62				// safe to call on a half-populated result.
63				if result.SourceURL != "" || result.TargetDir != "" {
64					printOutput(jsonOutput, result)
65				}
66				if err != nil {
67					return fmt.Errorf("convert-sha256: %w", err)
68				}
69				return nil
70			},
71		}
72	
73		cmd.Flags().StringVar(&req.SourceURL, "source-url", "", "source repository URL")
74		cmd.Flags().BoolVar(&req.SourceFollowInfoRefsRedirect, "source-follow-info-refs-redirect",
75			envBool("GITSYNC_SOURCE_FOLLOW_INFO_REFS_REDIRECT"),
76			"send follow-up source RPCs to the final /info/refs redirect host")
77		cmd.Flags().StringVar(&req.SourceAuth.Token, "source-token",
78			envOr("GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN", ""), "source token/password")
79		cmd.Flags().StringVar(&req.SourceAuth.Username, "source-username",
80			envOr("GITSYNC_SOURCE_USERNAME", "git"), "source basic auth username")
81		cmd.Flags().StringVar(&req.SourceAuth.BearerToken, "source-bearer-token",
82			envOr("GITSYNC_SOURCE_BEARER_TOKEN", ""), "source bearer token")
83		cmd.Flags().BoolVar(&req.SourceAuth.SkipTLSVerify, "source-insecure-skip-tls-verify",
84			envBool("GITSYNC_SOURCE_INSECURE_SKIP_TLS_VERIFY"),
85			"skip TLS certificate verification for the source")
86		cmd.Flags().StringVar(&req.TargetDir, "target-dir", "", "directory to initialize as a SHA256 bare repository")
87	
88		allRefsFlag(cmd, allRefsUsageScopeOnly, &req.AllRefs)
89		excludeRefPrefixFlag(cmd, &req.ExcludeRefPrefixes)
90		cmd.Flags().BoolVar(&req.IncludePullRefs, "include-pull-refs", false,
91			"with --all-refs, also convert server-internal pull/merge-request refs (refs/pull/*, refs/pull-requests/*, refs/merge-requests/*); off by default because they hold unmerged foreign code")
92		addProtocolFlag(cmd, &protocolVal)
93		cmd.Flags().BoolVarP(&req.Verbose, "verbose", "v", false, "verbose logging")
94		cmd.Flags().BoolVar(&req.Progress, "progress", false,
95			"show live per-phase object counts on stderr (TTY only)")
96		cmd.Flags().BoolVar(&req.Check, "check", false,
97			"verify the output after conversion (config, HEAD, refs, git fsck --full)")
98		cmd.Flags().StringVar(&req.SignMode, "sign-mode", sha256convert.SignModeNone,
99			"post-conversion signing: `none` (default), or `tips` to sign each branch tip as refs/tags/converted/<branch> via `git tag -s`")
100		cmd.Flags().StringVar(&req.SignKey, "sign-key", "",
101			"signing key id to pass to `git tag -s -u`; default uses the repo's user.signingkey")
102		cmd.Flags().BoolVar(&req.KeepSourceObjects, "keep-source-objects", false,
103			"keep the temporary SHA1 store on disk after conversion (for debugging)")
104		cmd.Flags().StringVar(&req.MappingFile, "write-mapping", "",
105			"write the full SHA1 → SHA256 mapping as a TSV to this path; useful for rewriting external references")
106		cmd.Flags().BoolVar(&req.SkipMessageRewrite, "no-rewrite-messages", false,
107			"do not rewrite SHA1 hash references found in commit and tag messages")
108		cmd.Flags().BoolVar(&req.SkipOriginNotes, "no-origin-notes", false,
109			"do not write a refs/notes/sha1-origin ref recording each commit's original SHA1")
110		cmd.Flags().BoolVar(&jsonOutput, "json", false, "print JSON output")
111	
112		return cmd
113	}
114	
115	// resolveConvertSHA256Args consumes positional args left-to-right,
116	// skipping fields the user already supplied via flags. Without that
117	// rule, `--source-url <url> <dir>` would look like one positional and
118	// land in SourceURL — leaving TargetDir empty even though the user
119	// gave both. The two-flags-no-positionals and zero-flags-two-positionals
120	// shapes also work, as do the symmetric --target-dir + positional URL.
121	func resolveConvertSHA256Args(req *sha256convert.Request, args []string) error {
122		positional := args
123		if req.SourceURL == "" && len(positional) > 0 {
124			req.SourceURL = positional[0]
125			positional = positional[1:]
126		}
127		if req.TargetDir == "" && len(positional) > 0 {
128			req.TargetDir = positional[0]
129		}
130		if req.SourceURL == "" || req.TargetDir == "" {
131			return errors.New("convert-sha256 requires a source URL and a target directory")
132		}
133		return nil
134	}
135

BUILD OK -> /tmp/git-sync-sha256test
convert-sha256 fetches a pack from a SHA1 HTTP source and writes a new
SHA256 bare repository on disk at <target-dir>. Every reachable object is
re-hashed under SHA256 and tree/commit/tag references are rewritten.

All branches and tags on the source are always converted — partial scope
risks stranding cross-branch references in commit messages. Pass
--all-refs to also include refs/notes/* and other custom namespaces;
pass --exclude-ref-prefix to subtract specific namespaces from --all-refs.
Exclude prefixes that would drop any branch or tag (e.g. refs/heads/feature/,
refs/tags/, refs/) are rejected at run time to preserve the always-convert
invariant.

Server-internal pull/merge-request refs (refs/pull/*, refs/pull-requests/*,
refs/merge-requests/*) are NOT converted even under --all-refs: they hold
unmerged code foreign to the repository, and mirroring the result onward
would republish it. Pass --include-pull-refs to convert them anyway.

The conversion is destructive in two ways the caller should be aware of:
GPG signatures on commits and tags are dropped (they sign over the
original SHA1 content and would be invalid post-rewrite), and any
submodule gitlink fails the run — its .gitmodules upstream still
advertises SHA1 hashes, so a rewritten SHA256 gitlink would point at a
hash the upstream cannot resolve and break `git submodule update` in
every clone. Exclude refs that reference submodules, or convert the
submodule repository first and re-point .gitmodules.

Usage:
  git-sync convert-sha256 [flags] <source-url> <target-dir>

Flags:
      --all-refs                           include every refs/* on the source (notes, pulls, custom namespaces) — scope only, no failure-handling effect
      --check                              verify the output after conversion (config, HEAD, refs, git fsck --full)
      --exclude-ref-prefix stringArray     exclude refs whose names start with this prefix; repeatable. Subtracts from auto-discovery (branches/tags/--all-refs); explicit --map values are not subject to this filter
  -h, --help                               help for convert-sha256
      --include-pull-refs                  with --all-refs, also convert server-internal pull/merge-request refs (refs/pull/*, refs/pull-requests/*, refs/merge-requests/*); off by default because they hold unmerged foreign code
      --json                               print JSON output
      --keep-source-objects                keep the temporary SHA1 store on disk after conversion (for debugging)
      --no-origin-notes                    do not write a refs/notes/sha1-origin ref recording each commit's original SHA1
      --no-rewrite-messages                do not rewrite SHA1 hash references found in commit and tag messages
      --progress                           show live per-phase object counts on stderr (TTY only)
      --protocol string                    protocol mode: auto, v1, or v2 (default "auto")
      --sign-key git tag -s -u             signing key id to pass to git tag -s -u; default uses the repo's user.signingkey
      --sign-mode none                     post-conversion signing: none (default), or `tips` to sign each branch tip as refs/tags/converted/<branch> via `git tag -s` (default "none")
      --source-bearer-token string         source bearer token
      --source-follow-info-refs-redirect   send follow-up source RPCs to the final /info/refs redirect host
      --source-insecure-skip-tls-verify    skip TLS certificate verification for the source
      --source-token string                source token/password
      --source-url string                  source repository URL
      --source-username string             source basic auth username (default "git")
      --target-dir string                  directory to initialize as a SHA256 bare repository
  -v, --verbose                            verbose logging
      --write-mapping string               write the full SHA1 → SHA256 mapping as a TSV to this path; useful for rewriting external references

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Live end-to-end test of convert-sha256 against a real public repo,
# including ephemeral SSH-signed attestation tags + verification.
set -uo pipefail

BIN=/tmp/git-sync-sha256test
SRC="${SRC:-https://github.com/spf13/cobra.git}"
WORK=$(mktemp -d)
OUT="$WORK/out"
MAP="$WORK/mapping.tsv"
echo "=== work dir: $WORK ==="
echo "=== source:   $SRC ==="

# --- ephemeral SSH signing key + git config so --sign-mode tips works ---
KEYDIR="$WORK/keys"
mkdir -p "$KEYDIR"
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N '' -f "$KEYDIR/id" -C sha256-test >/dev/null 2>&1
SIGNERS="$KEYDIR/allowed_signers"
echo "sha256-test $(cat "$KEYDIR/id.pub")" > "$SIGNERS"
export GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL="$WORK/gitconfig"
cat > "$GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL" <<EOF
[user]
    name = sha256 test
    email = sha256-test@example.com
    signingkey = $KEYDIR/id.pub
[gpg]
    format = ssh
[gpg "ssh"]
    allowedSignersFile = $SIGNERS
[commit]
    gpgsign = false
EOF

echo
echo "########## CONVERT (--check --sign-mode tips --write-mapping --json) ##########"
time "$BIN" convert-sha256 --check --sign-mode tips --write-mapping "$MAP" --json "$SRC" "$OUT" \
    > "$WORK/result.json" 2> "$WORK/stderr.log"
RC=$?
echo "exit code: $RC"
echo "----- stderr (phase lines) -----"
cat "$WORK/stderr.log"
echo "----- result.json -----"
cat "$WORK/result.json"

echo
echo "########## INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION ##########"
echo "----- object format -----"
git -C "$OUT" rev-parse --show-object-format
echo "----- config extensions -----"
git -C "$OUT" config --get extensions.objectformat
echo "----- ref count -----"
echo "heads: $(git -C "$OUT" for-each-ref refs/heads | wc -l)  tags: $(git -C "$OUT" for-each-ref refs/tags | wc -l)"
echo "----- git fsck --full (independent) -----"
git -C "$OUT" fsck --full 2>&1 | tail -20
echo "fsck exit: ${PIPESTATUS[0]}"
echo "----- HEAD -----"
git -C "$OUT" symbolic-ref HEAD
git -C "$OUT" rev-parse HEAD
echo "----- sha256-origin notes sample -----"
git -C "$OUT" log --format='%H' -n 1 HEAD | while read -r h; do
  echo "sha256 tip: $h"
  git -C "$OUT" notes --ref=sha1-origin show "$h" 2>&1 | head -2
done
echo "----- mapping file head -----"
head -3 "$MAP"
echo "mapping lines: $(wc -l < "$MAP")"
echo "----- verify a converted attestation tag -----"
TAG=$(git -C "$OUT" for-each-ref --format='%(refname)' 'refs/tags/converted/*' | head -1)
echo "tag: $TAG"
git -C "$OUT" cat-file -p "$TAG" | head -15
echo "----- git verify-tag -----"
git -C "$OUT" verify-tag "${TAG#refs/tags/}" 2>&1 | head -5
echo "verify-tag exit: ${PIPESTATUS[0]}"
echo
echo "=== DONE; artifacts in $WORK ==="
echo "WORKDIR=$WORK"

1053	func discoverReachable(ctx context.Context, src storer.Storer, roots []plumbing.Hash, progress *atomic.Int64) (map[plumbing.Hash]plumbing.ObjectType, error) {
1054		srcFS, ok := src.(*filesystem.Storage)
1055		if !ok {
1056			return nil, fmt.Errorf("source storage is not filesystem-backed (%T)", src)
1057		}
1058		reachable := make(map[plumbing.Hash]plumbing.ObjectType)
1059	
1060		// Iterative DFS with an explicit stack. The previous recursive
1061		// implementation walked deep linear histories (50k–100k commits
1062		// is not unheard of) one Go stack frame deep per parent edge,
1063		// growing the goroutine stack by tens of MiB on kernel-scale
1064		// runs. The explicit stack keeps memory usage proportional to
1065		// the in-flight frontier, not the longest chain.
1066		stack := make([]plumbing.Hash, 0, len(roots))
1067		stack = append(stack, roots...)
1068		for len(stack) > 0 {
1069			// Per-object cancellation check. Discovery on a kernel-scale
1070			// repo runs for several minutes before translate() takes
1071			// over, so without this Ctrl-C would not interrupt the run
1072			// until the discovery phase finished on its own.
1073			if err := ctx.Err(); err != nil {
1074				return nil, fmt.Errorf("discover: %w", err)
1075			}
1076			sha1 := stack[len(stack)-1]
1077			stack = stack[:len(stack)-1]
1078			if _, seen := reachable[sha1]; seen {
1079				continue
1080			}
1081			obj, err := srcFS.EncodedObject(plumbing.AnyObject, sha1)
1082			if err != nil {
1083				return nil, fmt.Errorf("discover %s: %w", sha1, err)
1084			}
1085			reachable[sha1] = obj.Type()
1086			if progress != nil {
1087				progress.Add(1)
1088			}
1089			switch obj.Type() { //nolint:exhaustive // OFSDelta/REFDelta/AnyObject/InvalidObject cannot reach a resolved storage.
1090			case plumbing.BlobObject:
1091				// No outgoing edges.
1092			case plumbing.TreeObject:
1093				tree := &object.Tree{}
1094				if err := tree.Decode(obj); err != nil {
1095					return nil, fmt.Errorf("discover decode tree %s: %w", sha1, err)
1096				}
1097				for _, e := range tree.Entries {
1098					if e.Mode == filemode.Submodule {
1099						// A submodule gitlink stores a hash that refers to a
1100						// commit in a *different* repository — the one named
1101						// by the matching .gitmodules URL. Even when that
1102						// commit happens to be in our source store, the URL
1103						// still points at an upstream SHA1 repo, so rewriting
1104						// the gitlink to SHA256 produces a tree that fsck-
1105						// passes but breaks `git submodule update` forever:
1106						// the upstream advertises only SHA1 hashes. The only
1107						// safe answer is to refuse and let the caller scope
1108						// the offending ref out (or convert the submodule
1109						// upstream first and re-point .gitmodules).
1110						return nil, fmt.Errorf(
1111							"tree %s contains a submodule gitlink %q at %s; convert-sha256 cannot rewrite submodule pointers "+
1112								"because the linked-to repository would still advertise SHA1 hashes — "+
1113								"exclude refs that reference it or convert the submodule repository first",
1114							sha1, e.Name, e.Hash)
1115					}
1116					stack = append(stack, e.Hash)
1117				}
1118			case plumbing.CommitObject:
1119				c := &object.Commit{}
1120				if err := c.Decode(obj); err != nil {
1121					return nil, fmt.Errorf("discover decode commit %s: %w", sha1, err)
1122				}
1123				stack = append(stack, c.TreeHash)
1124				stack = append(stack, c.ParentHashes...)
1125			case plumbing.TagObject:
1126				tag := &object.Tag{}
1127				if err := tag.Decode(obj); err != nil {
1128					return nil, fmt.Errorf("discover decode tag %s: %w", sha1, err)
1129				}
1130				stack = append(stack, tag.Target)
1131			default:
1132				return nil, fmt.Errorf("unexpected object type %v for %s during discovery", obj.Type(), sha1)
1133			}
1134		}
1135		return reachable, nil
1136	}
1137	
1138	// translate is intentionally recursive. Unlike discoverReachable's
1139	// purely-structural DFS, translate's edges are dynamic: tree entries,
1140	// commit parents, tag targets, *and* message-reference edges resolved
1141	// against the partial mapping built so far. Converting that to an
1142	// explicit work stack would require an "after-children" callback per
1143	// object type and is easy to get subtly wrong (re-encoding before all
1144	// referenced hashes are placed silently corrupts the message rewrite).
1145	//
1146	// Recursion depth is bounded by the longest dependency chain in the
1147	// source DAG — in practice the longest commit-parent chain, since
1148	// trees and tags add at most one frame each. Linux kernel history is
1149	// O(70k) commits along its deepest single-parent path; Go's growable
1150	// stacks comfortably absorb that (~tens of MiB). Cycle detection above
1151	// turns any unexpected graph shape into a clear error rather than a
1152	// stack-overflow crash.

533	func signBranchTips(ctx context.Context, out io.Writer, targetDir, signKey, sourceURL string, desired map[plumbing.ReferenceName]planner.DesiredRef) ([]string, error) {
534		gitBin, err := exec.LookPath("git")
535		if err != nil {
536			return nil, fmt.Errorf("git binary required to sign: %w", err)
537		}
538		// Iterate in a deterministic order so re-runs over the same source
539		// produce the same sequence of tags (modulo the signature payload,
540		// which carries the signer's timestamp).
541		branchNames := make([]string, 0, len(desired))
542		for name := range desired {
543			if name.IsBranch() {
544				branchNames = append(branchNames, string(name))
545			}
546		}
547		sort.Strings(branchNames)
548	
549		var signed []string
550		for _, refName := range branchNames {
551			shortName := plumbing.ReferenceName(refName).Short()
552			tagName := strings.TrimPrefix(attestationTagPrefix, "refs/tags/") + shortName
553			fmt.Fprintf(out, "signing %s ...\n", "refs/tags/"+tagName)
554	
555			msg := fmt.Sprintf(
556				"SHA1 → SHA256 conversion attestation for %s.\n\n"+
557					"Source: %s\nProduced by git-sync convert-sha256.\n",
558				refName, sourceURL)
559			args := []string{"-C", targetDir, "tag", "-s", "-m", msg}
560			if signKey != "" {
561				args = append(args, "-u", signKey)
562			}
563			args = append(args, tagName, refName)
564	
565			cmd := exec.CommandContext(ctx, gitBin, args...)
566			// Deliberate departure from the req.Out plumbing the rest of
567			// Run uses: gpg/ssh-agent and pinentry need a real TTY for
568			// passphrase prompts, so we inherit the parent's stdio
569			// directly. The consequence is that callers passing
570			// req.Out = io.Discard (e.g. tests) still see subprocess
571			// output on real stderr — that's the cost of working
572			// authentication.
573			cmd.Stdin = os.Stdin
574			cmd.Stdout = os.Stderr // git tag -s is usually quiet on success
575			cmd.Stderr = os.Stderr
576			if err := cmd.Run(); err != nil {
577				return signed, fmt.Errorf("git tag -s %s: %w", tagName, err)
578			}
579			signed = append(signed, "refs/tags/"+tagName)
580		}
581		return signed, nil
582	}
583	
584	// runChecks performs lightweight verification of the converted repo.
585	// Returns one Check per step. Callers print and/or fail-on-error based
586	// on these. No early return so users see the full picture even when an
587	// earlier check fails.
588	//
589	// sideOutputs holds the exact refs the run created on top of the
590	// source set (the origin-notes ref, any --sign-mode tips attestation tags), so
591	// the refs check can omit them from the resolved/expected fraction
592	// without false-positive-skipping a same-named source ref.
593	//
594	// hasBranches says whether any refs/heads/* landed in the target. If
595	// false, this is a tags-only conversion and HEAD is left at the
596	// PlainInit default (refs/heads/master, which won't exist); the HEAD
597	// check is then a no-op rather than a guaranteed failure.
598	func runChecks(ctx context.Context, targetDir string, repo *git.Repository, refsExpected int, sideOutputs map[plumbing.ReferenceName]struct{}, hasBranches bool) []Check {

1	// Package sha256convert implements a one-off SHA1 → SHA256 conversion for a
2	// single repository. It fetches a pack from a remote SHA1 HTTP endpoint into
3	// a temporary on-disk SHA1 bare repo, then walks every reachable object and
4	// re-emits it under SHA256 into a new bare repo at the user-supplied path.
5	//
6	// The tool is intentionally scoped: no hash mapping is persisted, GPG
7	// signatures on commits and tags are dropped (they sign over the original
8	// SHA1 byte stream and would be invalid post-rewrite), and any submodule
9	// gitlink fails the run so the caller chooses which refs to exclude. The
10	// linked-to repository's URL still points at an upstream SHA1 store,
11	// which has no way to resolve a SHA256-rewritten gitlink, so rewriting
12	// would produce a tree that fsck-passes but breaks
13	// `git submodule update`.
14	package sha256convert
15	
16	import (
17		"bufio"
18		"bytes"
19		"context"
20		"errors"
21		"fmt"
22		"io"
23		"net/http"
24		"net/url"
25		"os"
26		"os/exec"
27		"path/filepath"
28		"regexp"
29		"sort"
30		"strconv"
31		"strings"
32		"sync/atomic"
33		"time"
34	
35		git "github.com/go-git/go-git/v6"
36		"github.com/go-git/go-git/v6/plumbing"
37		"github.com/go-git/go-git/v6/plumbing/filemode"
38		formatcfg "github.com/go-git/go-git/v6/plumbing/format/config"
39		"github.com/go-git/go-git/v6/plumbing/object"
40		"github.com/go-git/go-git/v6/plumbing/storer"
41		transporthttp "github.com/go-git/go-git/v6/plumbing/transport/http"
42		"github.com/go-git/go-git/v6/storage/filesystem"
43	
44		gitsync "entire.io/entire/git-sync"
45		"entire.io/entire/git-sync/internal/auth"
46		"entire.io/entire/git-sync/internal/convert"
47		"entire.io/entire/git-sync/internal/gitproto"
48		"entire.io/entire/git-sync/internal/planner"
49	)
50	
51	// Request describes a single SHA1 → SHA256 conversion.
52	//
53	// Scope is intentionally fixed: every branch and every annotated/lightweight
54	// tag on the source is always converted. Partial scope risks stranding
55	// cross-branch references in commit messages, which defeats the point of a
56	// one-off cutover. AllRefs additionally pulls in refs/notes and other custom
57	// namespaces; ExcludeRefPrefixes subtracts from that. Server-internal
58	// pull/merge-request namespaces are excluded from AllRefs by default (see
59	// IncludePullRefs) because they carry unmerged foreign code.
60	type Request struct {
61		SourceURL                    string
62		SourceAuth                   gitsync.EndpointAuth
63		SourceFollowInfoRefsRedirect bool
64		TargetDir                    string
65	
66		AllRefs            bool
67		ExcludeRefPrefixes []string
68	
69		// IncludePullRefs opts back into converting the server-internal
70		// pull/merge-request namespaces (refs/pull/*, refs/pull-requests/*,
71		// refs/merge-requests/*) that AllRefs would otherwise pull in. They
72		// are excluded by default: those refs hold code proposed from forks
73		// and other branches — foreign to the repository until merged — and
74		// the converted repo is typically mirrored onward with
75		// `git push --mirror`, where a destination forge may surface them as
76		// ordinary refs and republish unreviewed code as repo content. No
77		// effect without AllRefs (the namespaces are out of scope anyway).
78		IncludePullRefs bool
79	
80		ProtocolMode gitsync.ProtocolMode
81		Verbose      bool
82		Progress     bool
83		Check        bool
84	
85		// SignMode selects the post-conversion attestation strategy. ""
86		// and SignModeNone sign nothing; SignModeTips runs
87		// `git tag -s converted/<branch> <tip>` for every converted branch,
88		// attesting the entire reachable history of each branch via its
89		// tip's parent chain. (A future "all" mode could sign every commit
90		// and tag.) SignKey is passed to git as `-u <SignKey>`; leave empty
91		// to use the repo's default signing identity.
92		SignMode string
93		SignKey  string
94	
95		KeepSourceObjects bool
96	
97		// MappingFile, when non-empty, is a path to which a TSV of every
98		// translated object's SHA1 → SHA256 mapping is written. Useful for
99		// rewriting external systems that reference old commit hashes.
100		MappingFile string
101	
102		// SkipMessageRewrite disables the inline rewrite of SHA1 hashes found
103		// in commit and tag messages. Off by default (rewriting is on).
104		SkipMessageRewrite bool
105	
106		// SkipOriginNotes disables the refs/notes/sha1-origin output that
107		// records each translated commit's original SHA1. Off by default
108		// (notes are written).
109		SkipOriginNotes bool
110	
111		// Out receives human-readable status lines. Nil means os.Stderr.
112		Out io.Writer
113	}
114	
115	// Counts tallies converted objects by kind.
116	type Counts struct {
117		Blobs   int `json:"blobs"`
118		Trees   int `json:"trees"`
119		Commits int `json:"commits"`
120		Tags    int `json:"tags"`
121	}
122	
123	// Result is the conversion summary, suitable for JSON output.
124	type Result struct {
125		SourceURL            string   `json:"sourceUrl"`
126		TargetDir            string   `json:"targetDir"`
127		Protocol             string   `json:"protocol"`
128		RefsConverted        int      `json:"refsConverted"`
129		Counts               Counts   `json:"counts"`
130		SignaturesStripped   int      `json:"signaturesStripped"`
131		MessageRewrites      int      `json:"messageRewrites"`
132		AmbiguousMessageRefs []string `json:"ambiguousMessageRefs,omitempty"`
133		SkippedPullRefs      int      `json:"skippedPullRefs,omitempty"`
134		OriginNotesRef       string   `json:"originNotesRef,omitempty"`
135		MappingFile          string   `json:"mappingFile,omitempty"`
136		SignedTags           []string `json:"signedTags,omitempty"`
137		Checks               []Check  `json:"checks,omitempty"`
138		TempDir              string   `json:"tempDir,omitempty"`
139	}
140	
141	// Check is one named verification step from --check, with the result
142	// and a short detail string suitable for logging/JSON output.
143	//
144	// Skipped distinguishes "this check passed" from "this check did not
145	// run" — e.g. fsck when git is not on PATH, or HEAD on a tags-only
146	// conversion. Skipped implies OK so callers that only branch on OK
147	// still treat it as non-fatal; callers that need a stricter signal
148	// (CI gating, audit logs) should branch on Skipped first.
149	type Check struct {
150		Name    string `json:"name"`
151		OK      bool   `json:"ok"`
152		Skipped bool   `json:"skipped,omitempty"`
153		Detail  string `json:"detail,omitempty"`
154	}
155	
156	// previewMax caps how many items from a potentially-long list (ambiguous
157	// prefixes, signed tags) are inlined into a Lines() summary before
158	// switching to a "(N more)" suffix.
159	const previewMax = 5
160	
161	// Lines satisfies the human-readable output contract used by other git-sync subcommands.
162	func (r Result) Lines() []string {
163		lines := []string{
164			"sha256 bare repo: " + r.TargetDir,
165			fmt.Sprintf("source: %s (%s)", r.SourceURL, r.Protocol),
166			fmt.Sprintf("converted: %d blobs, %d trees, %d commits, %d tags",
167				r.Counts.Blobs, r.Counts.Trees, r.Counts.Commits, r.Counts.Tags),
168			fmt.Sprintf("refs written: %d", r.RefsConverted),
169		}
170		if r.SignaturesStripped > 0 {
171			// Mixes commit/tag signatures (GPG/SSH/X.509) and embedded
172			// mergetag headers — each counts as one signed artifact whose
173			// signature became invalid post-rewrite.
174			lines = append(lines, fmt.Sprintf("warning: stripped %d signature(s) / mergetag header(s); they no longer match the rewritten object content", r.SignaturesStripped))
175		}
176		if r.MessageRewrites > 0 {
177			lines = append(lines, fmt.Sprintf("rewrote %d SHA1 hash reference(s) in commit/tag messages", r.MessageRewrites))
178		}
179		if n := len(r.AmbiguousMessageRefs); n > 0 {
180			preview := r.AmbiguousMessageRefs
181			extra := 0
182			if len(preview) > previewMax {
183				extra = len(preview) - previewMax
184				preview = preview[:previewMax]
185			}
186			line := fmt.Sprintf("warning: %d ambiguous SHA1 hex prefix(es) in messages left unrewritten (look up via the mapping file): %s",
187				n, strings.Join(preview, ", "))
188			if extra > 0 {
189				line += fmt.Sprintf(", ... (%d more)", extra)
190			}
191			lines = append(lines, line)
192		}
193		if r.SkippedPullRefs > 0 {
194			lines = append(lines, fmt.Sprintf("excluded %d foreign pull/merge-request ref(s) (refs/pull/*, refs/pull-requests/*, refs/merge-requests/*) from --all-refs; pass --include-pull-refs to convert them", r.SkippedPullRefs))
195		}
196		if r.OriginNotesRef != "" {
197			lines = append(lines, fmt.Sprintf("origin notes ref: %s (use `git notes --ref=%s show <sha256>` to recover old SHA1)",
198				r.OriginNotesRef, strings.TrimPrefix(r.OriginNotesRef, "refs/notes/")))
199		}
200		if r.MappingFile != "" {
201			lines = append(lines, "mapping written to: "+r.MappingFile)
202		}
203		if n := len(r.SignedTags); n > 0 {
204			preview := r.SignedTags
205			extra := 0
206			if len(preview) > previewMax {
207				extra = len(preview) - previewMax
208				preview = preview[:previewMax]
209			}
210			line := fmt.Sprintf("signed %d branch attestation tag(s): %s",
211				n, strings.Join(preview, ", "))
212			if extra > 0 {
213				line += fmt.Sprintf(", ... (%d more; full list in --json)", extra)
214			}
215			lines = append(lines, line)
216		}
217		if r.TempDir != "" {
218			lines = append(lines, "kept source objects: "+r.TempDir)
219		}
220		return lines
221	}
222	
223	// Run performs the conversion described by req.
224	//
225	//nolint:maintidx // Run is a linear orchestrator over distinct phases (fetch → discover → init → translate → refs → notes → mapping → sign → check); each phase is short and isolated. Splitting into helpers would obscure the pipeline rather than clarify it.
226	func Run(ctx context.Context, req Request) (Result, error) {

content.text: I've now read the full core file. The types and `Lines()` output are clean. My main independent finding is a `cleanupTarget` lifecycle inconsistency: a complete, valid conversion gets **deleted** if an optional post-conversion step (notes / mapping-file / signing) fails — yet the `--check` path deliberately keeps it. Let me confirm this empirically with a forced mapping-write failure on a tiny repo.

input.command:
cd /tmp && rm -rf sha256-cleanup-test && mkdir sha256-cleanup-test && cd sha256-cleanup-test
# Make a path where MkdirAll will fail: a regular file standing in for a dir component.
touch blockfile
OUT=/tmp/sha256-cleanup-test/out
echo "=== Running conversion with an unwritable --write-mapping path ==="
GIT_TERMINAL_PROMPT=0 /tmp/git-sync-sha256test convert-sha256 \
  --write-mapping /tmp/sha256-cleanup-test/blockfile/mapping.tsv \
  https://github.com/octocat/Hello-World.git "$OUT" 2>&1 | tail -25
echo
echo "=== exit: $? ==="
echo "=== Does the fully-converted target dir still exist? ==="
if [ -d "$OUT" ]; then
  echo "TARGET EXISTS — objects: $(find "$OUT/objects" -type f 2>/dev/null | wc -l | tr -d ' ')"
  git -C "$OUT" rev-parse --show-object-format 2>&1
  git -C "$OUT" for-each-ref --format='%(refname)' 2>&1 | head
else
  echo "TARGET DIR WAS DELETED  <-- the complete conversion was wiped because mapping-file write failed"
fi

content.text:
**Confirmed empirically.** The conversion fully succeeded (4 blobs / 4 trees / 5 commits / 3 refs / notes written — all printed), but because `--write-mapping` pointed at an unwritable path, the **entire converted repo was deleted**. The user is even told "sha256 bare repo: /tmp/.../out" on the line above, then it's gone. This is a real, reproducible issue.

Let me read the docs myself for an independent assessment while the review workflow finishes.

content.content:
1	# SHA1 → SHA256 Conversion
2	
3	`git-sync convert-sha256` is a one-off migration command that fetches a pack
4	from a SHA1 HTTP source and writes a new SHA256 bare repository on disk.
5	Every reachable object is re-hashed under SHA256 and tree, commit, and tag
6	references are rewritten accordingly. The command does not push to a
7	remote, does not modify the source, and is meant to run once per repo.
8	SHA256 hashes have no relation to the original SHA1 hashes beyond a
9	mapping the command can optionally emit.
10	
11	## Quick Start
12	
13	```bash
14	git-sync convert-sha256 \
15	  https://github.com/source-org/source-repo.git \
16	  /path/to/out.git
17	```
18	
19	The target directory must not exist or must be empty. The result is a bare
20	repository with `extensions.objectformat = sha256` and a
21	`refs/notes/sha1-origin` ref recording each commit's pre-conversion SHA1.
22	
23	Scope is fixed: every branch and every tag on the source is always
24	converted. Pass `--all-refs` to also include `refs/notes/*` and other
25	custom namespaces; pair with `--exclude-ref-prefix` to subtract specific
26	namespaces. Server-internal pull/merge-request refs (`refs/pull/*`,
27	`refs/pull-requests/*`, `refs/merge-requests/*`) are excluded even under
28	`--all-refs` — see [Sharp Edges](#sharp-edges). Pass `--include-pull-refs`
29	to convert them anyway.
30	
31	For a private source, pass the token via the environment so it isn't
32	exposed in `ps`:
33	
34	```bash
35	GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN=ghp_xxx git-sync convert-sha256 \
36	  https://github.com/source-org/private-repo.git \
37	  /path/to/out.git
38	```
39	
40	## What It Does
41	
42	1. Probes the source via smart HTTP and lists every in-scope ref.
43	2. Fetches a single self-contained pack via `upload-pack` into a
44	   temporary on-disk SHA1 bare repo (cleaned up at the end unless
45	   `--keep-source-objects` is passed).
46	3. Discovers every reachable object — walking trees, commits, and tags
47	   — and records each one's SHA1 and object type. Submodule gitlinks
48	   are checked here; unresolvable ones fail-fast before any output is
49	   written.
50	4. Initializes the target as a bare SHA256 repository
51	   (`git init --object-format=sha256` equivalent).
52	5. Translates every reachable object in topological order via memoized
53	   DFS:
54	   - **Blobs**: re-hashed under SHA256; content unchanged.
55	   - **Trees**: each entry's hash translated.
56	   - **Commits**: `tree` and `parent` hashes translated; GPG signatures
57	     and `mergetag` headers dropped; in-scope SHA1 references in the
58	     message are translated first and then substituted.
59	   - **Tags**: target hash translated; signatures dropped; message
60	     hashes rewritten the same way.
61	6. Writes refs at the translated tip hashes; repoints HEAD to the
62	   source's symbolic HEAD; builds `refs/notes/sha1-origin` (unless
63	   `--no-origin-notes`); emits the `--write-mapping` TSV (if requested).
64	
65	## Side Outputs
66	
67	The conversion deliberately decouples SHA1 from SHA256 — two runs of
68	this tool against the same source produce SHA256 hashes that share
69	nothing with the originals. Three on-ramps help bridge the gap.
70	
71	### Inline message rewriting (default on)
72	
73	Commit and tag messages are scanned for 7-to-40-character hex runs.
74	When a run uniquely matches a commit or tag SHA1 in the reachable set,
75	it is replaced with the full SHA256 hex:
76	
77	```
78	Reverts: a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a3b4c5d6e7f8a9b0    →    full SHA256
79	Cherry-picked from a1b2c3d                            →    full SHA256
80	```
81	
82	Two properties make this robust:
83	
84	- **Uniqueness is decided against the reachable set, not the in-flight
85	  mapping.** The discovery pass enumerates every reachable SHA1 before
86	  any encoding starts, so abbreviated prefixes get the same verdict
87	  regardless of how far the translation has progressed. Ambiguous
88	  prefixes are left unrewritten and reported (warning on stderr +
89	  `--json`'s `ambiguousMessageRefs`); look them up in the mapping file.
90	- **Cross-branch references resolve.** Each in-scope SHA1 mentioned in
91	  a message is added as a dependency edge in the translation DFS, so
92	  the referenced commit is translated before the referencing commit is
93	  encoded. A cherry-pick from a sibling branch resolves just as
94	  reliably as a revert of an ancestor.
95	
96	False positives are essentially impossible: a run is substituted only
97	if its prefix uniquely matches a commit or tag in scope. Blob and tree
98	hashes are excluded from the match set. Disable with
99	`--no-rewrite-messages` if you prefer untouched messages.
100	
101	### Origin notes ref (default on)
102	
103	`refs/notes/sha1-origin` holds, for each translated commit, the
104	pre-conversion SHA1 keyed by the new SHA256:
105	
106	```bash
107	git -C /path/to/out.git notes --ref=sha1-origin show <sha256>
108	# prints the original SHA1
109	
110	git -C /path/to/out.git log --notes=sha1-origin
111	# shows the original SHA1 below each commit's body
112	```
113	
114	Notes attach meaningfully only to commits; blobs, trees, and tags are
115	not represented. Disable with `--no-origin-notes`.
116	
117	### Sidecar mapping file (opt in via `--write-mapping`)
118	
119	`--write-mapping <path>` emits a TSV with one line per translated
120	object, sorted by SHA1:
121	
122	```
123	# sha1   sha256
124	00027b675386b21c4ca05316145671fb7034d251   d80415fa21bebb...
125	000bb155604d06f1c48fc7feb4b025d991ef3366   a23cf98db5abfa...
126	...
127	```
128	
129	Useful for bulk rewriting external systems: feed the file to a script
130	that walks Jira tickets, PR bodies, deploy manifests, or any other
131	system that holds frozen SHA1 references.
132	
133	### Branch-tip attestation tags (opt in via `--sign-mode tips`)
134	
135	`--sign-mode` defaults to `none` (sign nothing). `--sign-mode tips`
136	shells out to `git tag -s converted/<branch> <tip>` for every
137	converted branch after the conversion completes. Each resulting
138	signed annotated tag is a cryptographic attestation by the converter
139	that the entire reachable history of that branch — every parent, tree,
140	and blob — is what the converter saw at conversion time. Anyone can
141	verify the chain afterwards with `git verify-tag refs/tags/converted/<branch>`.
142	
143	The mechanism is the standard one: parent hashes are part of each
144	commit's bytes, so the tip's hash transitively commits to the whole
145	history. Signing the tip attests every ancestor.
146	
147	Important nuance: the signature is by the *converter*, not by the
148	original authors (whose own signatures are necessarily lost — see
149	"GPG signatures are stripped" under Sharp Edges). The attestation
150	chain becomes "*X attests this is the conversion they produced*"
151	rather than "*the original authors wrote this commit*". For internal
152	mirrors or single-identity repos that's a strict improvement over
153	unsigned-everywhere; for broad public repos it is weaker than the
154	pre-conversion chain.
155	
156	Signing uses the target repo's git signing config (`user.signingkey`,
157	`gpg.format`) by default — same as `git commit -S` or a normal
158	`git tag -s`. Override with `--sign-key <id>`, which is passed to
159	`git tag -s -u <id>`. SSH signing (`gpg.format = ssh`) and OpenPGP
160	both work because we shell out to `git`.
161	
162	Requires the `git` binary on `PATH`. Signing failures (no key
163	configured, gpg/ssh-agent unavailable, etc.) abort the run after the
164	conversion has already completed — the target repo is left in a
165	valid converted state, just without the attestation tags. Re-run
166	`git tag -s converted/<branch> <tip>` manually once the signing
167	identity is set up.
168	
169	## Flags
170	
171	```
172	--source-url                       source repository URL
173	--source-token                     source password/token (prefer env)
174	--source-username                  source basic auth username (default git)
175	--source-bearer-token              source bearer token
176	--source-insecure-skip-tls-verify  skip TLS verification (testing only)
177	--source-follow-info-refs-redirect follow /info/refs cross-host redirects
178	--target-dir                       SHA256 bare repo directory (must be empty)
179	
180	--all-refs                         also include refs/* outside heads/tags
181	                                   (notes, custom namespaces; excludes
182	                                   pull/merge-request refs by default)
183	--exclude-ref-prefix               subtract refs by prefix; repeatable
184	--include-pull-refs                with --all-refs, also convert
185	                                   refs/pull/*, refs/pull-requests/*,
186	                                   refs/merge-requests/* (off by default)
187	
188	--protocol                         protocol mode (auto, v1, v2)
189	--write-mapping                    write SHA1 → SHA256 TSV to this path
190	--no-rewrite-messages              skip inline hash rewrites in messages
191	--no-origin-notes                  skip refs/notes/sha1-origin
192	--check                            verify the output (config, HEAD, refs, git fsck)
193	--sign-mode                        signing mode: none (default) or tips
194	                                   (sign each branch tip as
195	                                   refs/tags/converted/<branch> via `git tag -s`)
196	--sign-key                         signing key id passed to `git tag -s -u <key>`
197	--keep-source-objects              leave the temp SHA1 store on disk
198	--progress                         live per-phase object counts (TTY only)
199	--json                             machine-readable output
200	--verbose, -v                      verbose logging
201	```
202	
203	There are no `--branch`, `--tags`, or `--map` flags: scope is fixed to
204	every branch and every tag on the source.
205	
206	Environment fallbacks: `GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN`, `GITSYNC_SOURCE_USERNAME`,
207	`GITSYNC_SOURCE_BEARER_TOKEN`, `GITSYNC_SOURCE_INSECURE_SKIP_TLS_VERIFY`,
208	`GITSYNC_SOURCE_FOLLOW_INFO_REFS_REDIRECT`, `GITSYNC_PROTOCOL`.
209	
210	## Sharp Edges
211	
212	**GPG signatures are stripped.** A signature is bytes signed over the
213	commit's pre-conversion content (including the SHA1 hashes in `tree`
214	and `parent` lines). After rewriting, the bytes no longer match the
215	signature, so verification would always fail; the command drops them
216	and prints a count. Signed annotated tags lose their signature the
217	same way. `mergetag` headers on merge commits — which embed a signed
218	tag with its own signature — are removed entirely, since the embedded
219	tag references original SHA1s and the signature was computed over
220	those original bytes.
221	
222	**Submodule gitlinks must resolve in-repo.** Tree entries with mode
223	`160000` reference a commit in another repository, but a SHA1 hash
224	cannot be embedded in a SHA256 tree. The command fails-fast in the
225	discovery pass — before the target bare repo is initialized — naming
226	the offending tree, entry, and hash. Convert the submodule repository
227	first so its commit hashes are available in SHA256.
228	
229	**Replace refs and source notes refs become detached.**
230	`refs/replace/<sha1>` encodes a SHA1 in the ref name, so the name
231	doesn't match under SHA256 and the replacement never triggers.
232	`refs/notes/*` trees from the source (copied under `--all-refs`)
233	encode the target object's hash as the entry name, so notes survive
234	as data but no longer attach to their original commits. Use the
235	tool's own `refs/notes/sha1-origin` for the inverse lookup.
236	
237	**Foreign pull/merge-request refs are excluded by default.** Even under
238	`--all-refs`, the command skips `refs/pull/*` (GitHub/Gitea/Forgejo),
239	`refs/pull-requests/*` (Bitbucket), and `refs/merge-requests/*` (GitLab).
240	These server-internal namespaces hold code proposed from forks and other
241	branches — content foreign to the repository's own history until it is
242	reviewed and merged. The converted repo is typically mirrored onward with
243	`git push --mirror`, and a destination forge may not treat those
244	namespaces as read-only PR refs; it can surface them as ordinary refs and
245	thereby republish unreviewed code as if it were part of the repository.
246	The run prints how many such refs it dropped. Pass `--include-pull-refs`
247	to convert them anyway (e.g. for a faithful archival mirror you control).
248	
249	## Operational Notes
250	
251	**One-off, not incremental.** Each run produces a fresh SHA256 repo
252	from scratch — there is no "fetch the new SHA1 commits and append to
253	the existing SHA256 repo" mode. Realistic use: convert once, then
254	make the converted repo the new canonical store. Branch and tag
255	hashes are deterministic across runs against the same source state;
256	only `refs/notes/sha1-origin` differs because its wrapper commit
257	carries `time.Now()` as the committer timestamp.
258	
259	**Loose-object storage.** Every translated object is written as a
260	loose file under `objects/<aa>/<rest>` — no pack file is produced.
261	Correct, but slow on filesystems that dislike millions of small files.
262	Run `git -C <target> gc --aggressive` afterwards to pack the converted
263	repo down to a single packfile.
264	
265	**Memory linear in reachable object count.** Two `map[Hash]…`
266	structures stay live for the whole run: `reachable` (SHA1 → object
267	type, built by discovery) and `mapping` (SHA1 → SHA256, built by
268	translation). At cobra scale (~5k objects), kilobytes; at Linux kernel
269	scale (~16M objects), roughly 2 GB peak.
270	
271	**Discovery adds a ~1.5× decode pass.** Every reachable object is
272	decoded twice: once in discovery (no encoding) and once in translation
273	(decode + encode). The cost buys consistent uniqueness verdicts for
274	message rewriting and submodule fail-fast.
275	
276	**Abbreviated-prefix lookup is a linear scan.** Each abbreviated SHA1
277	in a message triggers an O(reachable) scan to check uniqueness. Fine
278	to ~100k commits; slower past that. A sorted-prefix index would make
279	it O(log N), an easy optimization if someone hits the wall.
280	
281	## Verifying the Output
282	
283	Pass `--check` and the command runs four sanity checks against the
284	converted repo at the end of the run, printing one line each:
285	
286	```
287	verifying output ...
288	  ✓ config: extensions.objectformat = sha256
289	  ✓ HEAD: ffe9fff421b77f2dcc049a95b3b8ba7b9da8976dd61bcf35e9fe2d993babc470
290	  ✓ refs: 37 / 37 resolve to objects
291	  ✓ git fsck --full: clean
292	```
293	
294	The checks are:
295	
296	1. **config** — `extensions.objectformat = sha256` is present in
297	   `<target>/config`.
298	2. **HEAD** — resolves to a non-zero hash and that object exists in
299	   the store.
300	3. **refs** — every written ref (except `refs/notes/sha1-origin`,
301	   counted separately) resolves to an object in the store. The count
302	   matches `RefsConverted`.
303	4. **git fsck --full** — the external `git` binary runs a full
304	   integrity check. Skipped (and reported as such) when `git` isn't
305	   on `PATH`; the conversion still succeeds.
306	
307	If any check fails the command exits non-zero. The full per-check
308	results are also in `--json`'s `checks` array. Without `--check` no
309	verification runs and the run completes as soon as the conversion
310	itself finishes.
311	
312	You can also run the checks by hand on a converted repo, with or
313	without `--check`:
314	
315	```bash
316	git -C /path/to/out.git fsck --full                     # zero errors expected
317	git -C /path/to/out.git config extensions.objectformat  # prints sha256
318	git -C /path/to/out.git log --oneline -5                # SHA256 hashes
319	git -C /path/to/out.git log --notes=sha1-origin -5      # with original SHA1
320	```
321	
322	To use the result as a working repo:
323	
324	```bash
325	git clone /path/to/out.git /path/to/checkout
326	```
327	
328	To serve it from a host that accepts SHA256:
329	
330	```bash
331	git -C /path/to/out.git push --mirror <new-remote-url>
332	```
333	
334	## Implementation Notes
335	
336	The pipeline runs in four phases (pack fetch → discovery → target init →
337	translation), with refs and side outputs written at the end. Submodule
338	errors surface in discovery, before the target repo is materialized.
339	
340	Translation is a memoized recursive DFS. Tree, parent, tag-target, and
341	message-reference edges are all part of the DFS, so the mapping is
342	populated by the time any object's bytes are encoded. A defensive
343	`inProgress` set guards against cycles; real Git histories can't form
344	them (parent/tree/tag-target edges are a DAG, and SHA1 message-
345	reference cycles are cryptographically infeasible), but a trip into
346	the guard becomes a hard error rather than a stack overflow.
347	
348	Translated objects are written with go-git's `SetEncodedObject`. Each
349	one is built through the target store's `NewEncodedObject`, which binds
350	it to the store's SHA256 hasher, so both the returned hash and the
351	on-disk loose path are computed under SHA256. (Earlier revisions wrote
352	loose objects by hand: `go-git/v6@v6.0.0-alpha.3`'s
353	`plumbing/format/objfile.Writer` hardcoded SHA1 in its hasher and would
354	have placed every translated object at a SHA1-derived path. That was
355	fixed upstream in `v6.0.0-alpha.4`, which derives the hash format from
356	the store config.) A unit test recomputes `sha256` of every loose
357	object's decompressed content and compares it against the filename to
358	guard against a regression.
